video_name: Video_04_LangChain Components ｜ GenAI using LangChain ｜ Video 2 ｜ CampusX.mp3
full_text: Hi guys, my name is Nitesh and you are welcome to my YouTube channel. In today's video, we are going to continue our LANG CHAIN playlist. Till now, I have added one video in this playlist and in that one video, I discussed what is a LANG CHAIN and I also tried to tell you why LANG CHAIN is needed. Now, today's video is more important than the previous video because in today's video, I am going to give you a very detailed overview of what are the core components in LANG CHAIN. Now, if you watch the whole video, you will get two advantages. First, you will get a very deep intuition that how the LANG CHAIN as a framework is organized. You will understand the thought process of those people who built this library. And the second advantage will be that going forward, the videos that I am going to put in this playlist, I will put it on the basis of today's video. Means, you will get to see detailed videos on the components that I have discussed in this video. So, by watching this video, you will get a roadmap of what we are going to cover in this playlist. Now, before starting the video, I would like to give a disclaimer that we are not going to do any code in today's video. We are not going to build any project and the reason behind it is very simple. I told you before starting this playlist that generally, I have seen that all the resources around LANG CHAIN are online. There, people start building projects before giving the foundation of LANG CHAIN. And I think that is not the right way to learn this library. I think the right way is that you first take a conceptual overview of the library and then start coding. So, I have planned the same in this playlist. I have planned the same in the previous video and in today's video. I am giving you a theoretical foundation about the library and once this foundation is set, then we will code in all the lectures and build projects. So, that is why I have planned not to include any kind of code in this video. But, this does not mean that this video will be boring. I have made sure that in the process of explaining any component, I will give you very interesting and relevant industrial examples. And most likely, you will not find this video boring. Now, I think I have given you enough introduction about this video. Let's start. Now guys, before starting the video, I would like to give you a quick recap of the previous video. The previous video was the first video of this playlist. And there, I tried to explain in detail what LANG CHAIN is and why it is needed. So, I had told you that LANG CHAIN is an open source framework, with the help of which you can make LLM powered applications. Then, we discussed why LANG CHAIN was needed. There, I had given you an example that we want to make such an application where anyone can talk to PDF. And then, I tried to explain to you through a system design that how complex it is to make such a system. Because there will be many components and there will be many interactions between those components. And if someone wants to code such a system with a scratch, then he will have to work very hard. And then, I had explained to you how LANG CHAIN comes in picture and solves this whole complexity. The biggest advantage of LANG CHAIN is that LANG CHAIN can make orchestration and pipeline build very efficiently between all these components. And you get maximum output in very minimal code. I had also told you that LANG CHAIN has the concept of chains. Where you can put different components in a chain. And the biggest beauty of chains is that the output of one component automatically works as an input for the other component. And we don't need to manually code this. I had told you one more feature that how LANG CHAIN is a model agnostic framework. Which means that if you don't use OpenAI GPT models and use GEMINI models of Google, then you don't need to change the code a lot. Literally, this work will be done in one to two lines of code. So, I had told you all these benefits in the last video. I had also told you that what people are doing to make use of LANG CHAIN in today's rate. I had told you in examples that many people are making conversational chatbots for their companies. Many people are making AI knowledge assistants. And many companies have now started making agents. So, we have done all this discussion in the last video. And I really hope you have seen that video. If you haven't seen that video, I would recommend you to go back and watch that video. After watching that video, you will understand today's video a little better. Okay, so this was the recap. Now let's move towards today's topic. Today's topic is that we want to learn what components are there in LANG CHAIN. So, first of all, I would like to show you this diagram. Because I have listed down all the components in this diagram. So, in total, LANG CHAIN has six different components. And if you have read these six components, trust me, you will understand the majority of the concepts of LANG CHAIN. So, the first component is models, the second is prompts, the third is chains, the fourth is memory, the fifth is indexes, and the sixth is agents. Our goal in this video is to discuss all these components in detail one by one. And you will notice in the playlist that we are going to talk about these six components in this entire playlist. So, now let's start with our first component, that is models. So guys, we will read the component of models very well because this is the most important component of LANG CHAIN. Here it is written that LANG CHAIN models are core interfaces through which you interact with AI models. Now I know that you won't understand much of this definition. So, to explain things well, I will tell you a little backstory. So, the history of NLP, everyone wanted to make a particular application and that is the chatbot. Chatbot is probably the most popular application of the NLP world. Everyone wants to make their own chatbot in NLP. But there were two major problems in making chatbot before, now there are no more. So, the first problem was to make the chatbot understand the user query. I typed hi, can you check my email? Now, I can explain to the chatbot what I mean. This was the first big challenge in making chatbot and we call it NLU. It is a challenge of natural language understanding. The second challenge is that even if the chatbot understands what I am asking it, it should give the right reply. This is also a big challenge. So, basically context aware text generation is also a big challenge. And a lot of effort was put into solving both these challenges. Eventually, LLMs came and LLMs solved both these problems together. So, to train LLMs, we used almost all the internet data. Not only did we develop an understanding of natural language in LLMs, but at the same time, we also developed the capabilities of context aware text generation. So, I mean to say that after LLMs came, these two big problems of NLP were solved together. But, a new problem came into the picture. The new problem was that since LLMs were trained on the internet data, there are a lot of parameters in them, like billions of parameters. Because of which, the size of LLMs is huge. The size of LLMs in the market is greater than 100 GB. The problem is that a normal person cannot run such big files on his computer. For that matter, small companies cannot run on their servers because the cloud bill will be so high that you will not be able to pay. So, this was the second big challenge after LLMs came. Then, this challenge was also solved with the help of APIs. So, the big companies like OpenAI, Google, what did they do? They kept the LLMs files on their servers and created an API around them. Now, what is it? Now, anyone in the world can hit these APIs, send their query and these APIs will talk to LLMs. LLMs will respond and the response will be received by the user. Now, what is the benefit of this model is that I don't have to run LLMs on my computer or on my Cloud services. I will send as many questions as I can to API and I have to pay the bill So this problem was solved But now a third problem came in the picture And that problem was of implementation Means different LLM providers wrote the APIs in different ways Which means if I am an application developer and I have to talk to two different LLM APIs in my application Then I have to write different types of code I will show you this thing Here two pieces of code are written This is the first code of OpenAI If I have to communicate with the GPT model of OpenAI Then this is its code And this code is of the Cloud API If I have to talk to the Cloud Sonnet of Anthropic Then this is the code to communicate with its API And you can already see that there is a slight difference So if I have to use both LLMs in the same application for different purposes Then I have to write two different types of code Or suppose I made an application and I made it in the beginning with the help of OpenAI And I wrote this code Now suddenly I don't have to use the APIs of OpenAI because they are costly And I have to use the APIs of the Cloud So I have to change my code base and convert it into this Which is a hard work So basically standardization became a challenge The APIs of Gemini are behaving differently The APIs of OpenAI are behaving differently The APIs of the Cloud are behaving differently Even the response I am getting is of a different type So Langchin identified this problem Langchin identified this problem And they said that what if we make such an interface With which anyone can talk to any company's API in a standardized fashion Means you don't have to make many changes in the code And you can communicate with OpenAI and Gemini by changing the code So this is the model component of Langchain Model component is basically an interface With the help of which you can communicate with any company's AI model In fact, I will show you the code now Look at the bottom Here both these pieces of code are of Langchain In the first code, we are talking to the API of OpenAI And in the second one, we are talking to the Cloud And now you go and see for yourself how much difference there is in the code Here you are calling a different package After that, the method of calling is exactly the same The method of printing the result of the flip is exactly the same You literally change one line In fact, just change two lines One line here and one line here And your work is done You can immediately switch from OpenAI to Cloud In fact, the answers you will get after flipping They will also be of a very similar type So it will be very easy to parse them So in short, the component of the model The AI model standardizes the entire interface of talking I hope you understood what the models component is Now I will discuss one more thing here In Langchain, you can communicate with two types of AI models The first one is language model And the second one is embedding model I will quickly tell you about these two So language models are basically LLMs Where you give a text input to these models Like how are you today And they give you a text output I am good, how about you So LLMs are basically language models Which work on text-in-text-out philosophy With their help, you can make all the applications You can make chatbots, AI agents You can do all this with the help of language model Embedding models are slightly different These are the kind of models that you give text as input But they give you a vector as output We discussed this in the last video Their main use case is semantic search I told you about this in the last video So Langchain can communicate with both these models With language model and embedding model I will show you the language models available in Langchain And the embedding models So if you go to documentation This is the Langchain documentation Here you go to chatmodels section And you will see all the providers below With which you can communicate with the help of Langchain Chat is Anthropic, Chat is Mistral AI Azure chat is OpenAI, Chat is Vertex AI Chat is Bedrock, this is AWS Chat is Hugging face So you will see many names here Which you have heard before Not only that, what features are available in those language models They are also telling you Like is the feature of tools calling available or not Tools calling is useful when you make an agent Structured output is available or not from the model Jason is getting output in the mode or not Can you run that model locally on your machine or not Can you provide multi-model input or not All these are given here So definitely go and study this list Which language models are available in Langchain Second, this page tells you Which embedding models are available in Langchain And again you will find out a lot of famous names Like OpenAI, Mistral AI And there are also embedding models of IBM There is Ulama So I would recommend you to go and explore this page And read this page, your perspective will be built In nutshell, if I can summarize So model component is basically an interface With the help of which you can communicate with AI model And the biggest problem solved by this interface Or component is that The previous APIs of LLM Were singing their own songs This particular component standardizes that So that you can change the code a little And talk to any LLM provider That is the whole idea behind this particular component I really hope you understood this Second component of Langchain is prompts Let's understand what prompts are So if you are working with any LLM So the input you send to that LLM Is called a prompt So prompts are basically inputs Provided to a LLM Like if you are working on chat GPT And you asked a question from chat GPT What is campusX So this string is what is campusX You can call it a prompt Now prompts are Very very important in the world of LLMs The output of LLM Depends a lot on prompts In fact it is very sensitive Means if you change the prompts a little The output of LLMs changes a lot For example, suppose you asked a LLM Explain linear regression In academic tone And then you asked the same LLM Explain linear regression in fun tone You will notice that you changed only one word But the output of your LLM Will be very different So prompts are super important In fact, in the last two years A field of study has been created around prompts You will also see jobs This field is called prompt engineering And this job profile is called prompt engineers Although it is a little defamed on social media But trust me this is an important field of study Around LLMs So Langchin also identified this thing Since we help in making LLM powered applications And LLMs depend a lot on prompts So we need a very good component To handle prompts And they developed a prompt component Langchain gives you a lot of flexibility To create prompts In fact you can create different and powerful prompts In Langchain I will give you some examples Like let's see the first example With the help of Langchain You can create very powerful dynamic and reusable prompts Like you don't know What topic the user will ask me to summarize And what tonality He will ask me to summarize So what I can do is I can create a dynamic prompt Where this is my prompt Summarize this topic in this tone I just put some placeholders here Like I didn't tell the topic now And I didn't tell the emotion now Now what I will do is I will ask the user The user will ask me to tell me about cricket In a fun tone So now what I can do is I can quickly Replace this placeholder with cricket And this placeholder with fun And I will give this placeholder to my LLM Similarly tomorrow another user can come And he will use this prompt for let's say biology In serious tone So this is how you can create dynamic and reusable prompts In Langchain Apart from this if you want You can also create role based prompts So what you can do is first you create a system level prompt Where you can write a message like Hi you are an experienced And you put a placeholder Of profession Profession will tell you later And then you create a user level prompt Where the user is telling me about this topic Now in future a user will come And he will say that you are an experienced doctor So this will fill the prompt here And the topic will be viral fever So what you are doing is You are guiding your LLM That you are an experienced doctor And now tell me about viral fever like an experienced doctor Similarly tomorrow another user will come And he will say Hi you are an experienced engineer So what is this about? I don't know something like that So you can also do role based prompting using Langchain Next example is also very interesting If you want you can do few shot prompting Using Langchain So what is few shot prompting? You show your LLM some examples first And then you ask a question to it So you are creating a chatbot for this quote So what you will do first You will show your LLM some examples Of previous messages And you will tell what type of message it is Like it is written here So this is a billing issue I have already told LLM this I have given one more example This is a technical issue This is a general enquiry So I have given examples like this And I have told what should be your output Now what I am doing is I am creating a template Where I will ask LLM questions in this format This will be my ticket as in my query And I have to tell category in output Now I will create a few shot prompt template Where I will give all my examples I will give my example prompt Where I will give my example template And now I will send a new query And I will ask Based on previous examples Tell me which category this new example falls in So now what will happen So here is my entire code My LLM will get this prompt Classify the following customer support tickets Into one of the following categories Billing issue, technical problem and general enquiry This is my example 1 This is my example 2 This is my example 3 And this is my final query And now my LLM will print my category And give it to me So this is a very good few shot prompt template So you can create this very easily Using LangChin Now at this point if you find this code difficult Because obviously you have not read all these things So don't worry, I just wanted to show you the concept How many different types of prompting techniques You can implement in LangChin with the help of prompt component So again a very important component And in future we will do a few dedicated videos on this Next component of LangChin is Chains And this is such an important component that The name of the chain is written on it So let me explain to you what chains are And what things can be done So basically there is a component With the help of which we can build pipelines in LangChin So basically the funda is That you can make any LLM application You can give it the shape of a pipeline Right? And the pipeline that will be made You can make it with the help of chains Ok, let me explain to you through an example You have to make an LLM application Where the user will give you an input With a big English text Of around 1000 words And what you have to do in the output You have to give its Hindi summary In less than 100 words You have to make this LLM application Ok? So you have decided that the flow of this LLM application Will be that you will get the input first You will get the input first You will send this input first To an LLM And the work of this LLM Is to translate this input into Hindi So here the work of translation is being done So here the work of translation is being done After that we will send this translated text To a second LLM And what this LLM will do It will generate its Hindi summary In less than 100 words So you have decided this flow for your LLM application Now what you can do is easily You can represent this flow in the form of a pipeline Ok? If you don't use this then what you have to do You have to do all this pipeline design manually Which means you will take the user's input Then you will call this LLM You will provide that input And you will tell it to translate it into Hindi You will get the Hindi translation And then you will pick it up And go to another LLM And generate the Hindi translation summary And then you will get your final output So if you design this pipeline manually Then you have to take the output from every state And put it in the input of the next stage manually Chains solve this problem Chains have the biggest feature That they automatically Make the output of one stage And you don't have to write a code manually Which means if you Made this whole task With the help of chain Then you have to simply give an English text And call the chain And behind the scenes This whole task will be automatically executed And you will get your result directly So you don't have to worry about the LLM1 Output being put in LLM2 Chains are doing all the heavy lifting behind the scenes So in simple words Chain is this concept With the help of which you can create a pipeline in the lang chain And the biggest beauty of this pipeline is That the output of the previous stage automatically Is the input of the next stage So this is the code for it Now the good thing is that This chain is a simple sequential chain Where one stage after the other But if you want You can make very complex pipelines with the help of chains Ok, I will give you some examples Like one pipeline that you can make Is of parallel chain I will rewrite it Parallel chain So what is in this Suppose what you want to do You want to make any one An application where the user Will give you an input And you have to print a detailed report On that input And then you have to give it to the user Suppose the user wrote 9-11 incident So you will get a detailed report in the output Now what you want to implement in this You want to combine multiple LLMs And show it Which means that your flow will be something like That you will get an input And you will send that input to LLM1 And LLM1 will Will generate a report on that topic Then you will simultaneously Send that input to LLM2 And LLM2 will also generate a report on it Then what you are doing You are sending it to a third LLM And you are asking them to combine both the reports And then the combined result You are showing the user as output Now this is an example of parallel chain Where you are executing parallel things So very easily you can execute this whole flow With the help of chains I will give you one more example One more example of conditional chains Where you based on a condition You can do different types of processing For example, you are making An AI agent Who receives feedback from the user Okay So you are asking the user How did you like our service User is telling you How did he like the feedback Okay Now you are processing this feedback With the help of an LLM If this feedback is good Then you are saying thank you And the work is done But if the feedback is bad Then you are immediately Sending an email to your customer support team Now this is also an application Which you can easily build And the processing is On a condition basis And this can also be implemented easily With the help of chains And you can make more complex pipelines Using the chains component of LLM It is very beautifully designed And it minimizes a lot of your hard work We will study the chains in detail The next component is indexes And honestly reading indexes will seem simple Because in the previous video I explained this topic in detail But nevertheless let's read the definition So it is written here Indexes connect your application to external knowledge Such as PDFs, websites and databases Okay Now what are indexes Before discussing this I want to tell you What are the things in indexes First is document loader Second is text splitter Third is vector store Fourth is retrievers These four things Are the indexes Now let's discuss in detail What are indexes and why they are needed So you use chat GPT For all your queries And chat GPT mostly tells you The answer for all your queries So most of the things are known But there are certain scenarios Where chat GPT won't be able to answer you For example I work in a company called XYZ Now if I go to chat GPT And ask chat GPT Can you tell me What is the leave policy Of my company XYZ Or I can ask What is the notice period policy Of my company XYZ So do you think chat GPT will be able to answer this question The answer is no Since these questions are asked On my company's private data Chat GPT won't be able to answer this Because chat GPT has not seen this data So this problem we all face That in our private And professional life The things we are doing We can't ask questions related to that from chat GPT Because chat GPT doesn't know about it So is there any solution The answer is yes What you can do is You can connect with Professional knowledge source Like what I can do is I can pick up an LLM And provide it to XYZ's Full rules book Now if I ask a normal question Like who is the Prime Minister of India Then LLM will give the answer Because he has seen all this in training But if I ask what is the leaf policy Of XYZ company Then he will give the answer Because he has external knowledge source So to build applications like this Indexes are used And its 4 main components are written here Now the good thing is I told you about this system in the last video I will show you the same example again So how is this system implemented What you do first Our PDF Our company's rule book Since it is very big, it is of 1000 pages It is a very big company, so there are many rules So what you have to do first You have to load the PDF from the file That is on Google Drive or S3 And the task of loading Is of document loader So you have to bring the data from anywhere Document loader will help you Now once you have brought the data Now what you do is Semantic search can be done on this document You break the whole document into small chunks On the basis of pages, paragraphs On the basis of chapters Like we are doing on the basis of pages So we have made 1000 chunks of 1000 pages Who is doing this chunking operation Our second component text splitter After that we convert our documents into vectors So that semantic search can be done And each of our pages will create embeddings Using some embedding model Which we discussed earlier Now I have 1000 pages corresponding 1000 embedding vectors Now obviously since I Can search anytime, today, day after I can do it after 10 days So I have to store these vectors somewhere So I store these vectors in a database And since we have to store vectors in this database This special database is called Vector database or vector store Which is our third component So now I have My entire embedding vector Stored in a vector store Now what happened is as soon as a user query came That tell me what is the leaf policy of XYZ company So here comes a fourth component Which is called Retriever Retriever quickly picks up this query And generates its embedding with the help of an embedding model And then the vector we get Holds that vector and does a semantic search In this database And from semantic search It gets relevant results And then it picks up the query of the user And gives it to LLM And then LLM replies to you So this whole thing is happening We execute it through indexes And in indexes, these four components together Do the heavy lifting So in simple words indexes is the way Using which you can build LLM applications Now this is external knowledge source Now external knowledge source can be anything It can be PDFs, it can be a website Or it can be a database of a company Here is the full flexibility So in the future we will do all this And show you in this playlist I will practically make proper projects And show you how this whole thing works And because of this, the whole thing becomes very easy So you don't need to write a lot of code So I really hope you got an idea about indexes The next component of the chain is memory Now you may understand it with the name But let's discuss it in detail Here I have written a statement, let's read it once And try to understand it Here it is written LLM API calls are stateless And it is a big problem too When you make LLM based applications You will understand that when you Make API calls in LLM All the calls are stateless What does this mean? I will explain it to you through an example Suppose I am using an LLM API Let's say I am using OpenAI APIs And I am interacting with GPT model And I sent this question to the model Who is Narendra Modi? So the model replied to me Narendra Modi is an Indian politician who is the current Prime Minister of India Now what I did is I changed my query And I wrote How old is he? Which means I am asking about his age Now I hit the same thing on the API And guess what? This is the result that I am getting As an AI, I don't have access to personal data about individuals Unless it has been shared with me Which means he doesn't remember The last question was asked about Narendra Modi So he is not able to decode here Now I guess you are understanding the meaning of this sentence And the meaning of this word So what is stateless? Stateless means whenever you call on LLM APIs And send requests Every request is independent Which means he doesn't have any memory about the previous request And this is a big problem Now think for yourself If you make a chatbot with this type of system Then talking to the chatbot will be very frustrating Because he will not have any memory of the conversation He will have to remind him every time What we were talking about And this is a big problem when you go to make LLM applications And this problem is solved by this component Which is called memory So with the help of memory You can add the memory feature in your conversation Now this is an advanced topic But still I will tell you a little bit About the different types of memories available in LLM So there are many different types of memories But the frequently used memories I have written here Like the most used memory is conversation buffer memory So what you do here is Like you are talking to your chatbot So as much as you have talked so far You store all of them And when you are making the next API call Then you send this whole chat history To the model Then he understands What is being talked about There is only one problem in this If your chat gets too big Then the chat history will also get very big And it will cost a lot of money to process the text So this is one type of memory Second is conversation buffer window memory Here you store last N interactions Like you said that store last 100 messages So this is constantly updated And at any point You keep the last 100 interactions with you And send it to the next API call After that the third memory is Summarizer based memory Here you generate a summary Of your chat history And you send that summary To your API call So we can save some text And we have to pay less There is another memory Where you store Specialized pieces of information In memory Like user preferences You keep these in memory So that you can talk more easily So this is a very interesting topic And honestly a very practical topic So we have discussed this topic And we will discuss more about this in detail Now let's talk about the last component Which is agents Agents is a component in the Lank chain With the help of which you can easily make AI agents Now if you talk about the last 6 months I am pretty sure you have heard about AI agents Somewhere or the other Everyone is saying that AI agents are going to be The next big thing Which can be true But we will try to understand from scratch What AI agents are And how you can build them in the Lank chain So we have discussed this multiple times That LLMs have two big features First is NLU And second is text generation Which means LLMs Can understand the language And can also generate the right text So obviously when LLMs came in the picture The most obvious use case Of LLMs was to make a chat board And people made a lot of chat boards Using LLMs In fact the most popular AI application today Chat GPT is a chat board So slowly people thought That if my chat board Can understand and reply my message Then it can also work Like I will explain you with an example Suppose I am on a travel website And I am talking to a chat board On a website I asked the chat board Can you tell me Which is the best travel destination In India during summer Since their chat board was based on an LLM And that LLM was trained on the internet data So in its training there will be information That during summer hill stations are the best tourist spots So the chat board replied quickly That you can go to Shimla or Manali Now if this was an AI agent Instead of a chat board Then I can also make it work Like I can ask this question That can you tell me On 24th January Which is the cheapest flight From Delhi to Shimla And what will this AI agent do It will quickly Hit an API And will get the answer That this flight is from Indigo Which is the cheapest On 24th January from Delhi to Shimla Now what can I do I can ask this question Can you book the flight And since it has this capability It will quickly go to Make My Trip website And will book our flight And this is the main difference between a chat bot And an AI agent It's like AI agent is Chat bot Chat bot can only talk AI agent can work And how does this happen I will tell you So AI agent basically has two things Which chat bot doesn't have First it has reasoning capability And second AI agent has Some tools Like hitting this API And finding out which flight is the cheapest So AI agent has different tools With these two capabilities An AI agent can work And give you So through an example I will explain How AI agent works exactly So suppose we have an AI agent And we have given it Two tools First tool is a calculator Means During any conversation If my AI agent wants to do any mathematical calculation Then he can use this tool And second I have given it It has given two accesses That if ever If you want to get weather condition of any city On any date, then it can get it I have given these two tools to my AI agent Now a user comes And he starts talking to my AI agent And he asks the question Can you multiply The temperature Or rather today's temperature Of Delhi With 3 This is the query User asked can you multiply Today's temperature of Delhi with 3 And tell me the answer Now see how AI agent will handle this query Since it has reasoning capability It means it can reason What exactly I have to do So here through different techniques Reasoning is done One of the popular techniques Chain of thought prompting Where your AI agent will do Will break down your query Step by step And then he will try to reason through it Like AI agent will read this query Can you multiply today's temperature of Delhi with 3 So he will break down this query step by step He will say that I have to multiply today's temperature of Delhi With 3 Means I need today's temperature of Delhi And as soon as I get the temperature I can multiply it with 3 Now his entire focus will come On taking out today's temperature of Delhi So he will go and check That does he have a tool that can tell him the temperature of Delhi So he will notice that yes He has a weather API So he will quickly hit the weather API and give Delhi in input And weather API will flip and say 25 degree Celsius So now your agent will say Now I have the temperature of Delhi Now I have to multiply this number 25 degree Celsius with 3 But to perform this operation I need a calculator So he will go back and check in his tools So he will call this calculator And give it in input 25, 3 and multiplication operation These 3 inputs will go to the calculator And will flip the calculator and say 75 And this 75 will be your output And this is how AI agents work So basically if I summarize So in AI agent and chatbot AI agent has reasoning capacity And tools have access So an AI agent is just An evolved form of chatbot Where you can perform some action with the help of AI agents Because it has got reasoning capabilities And access to tools I hope you understood It is a very interesting topic And I am pretty sure All the big companies and researchers In the world of AI are converting to this topic And I am pretty sure In the next 1-1.5 years, there will be a lot of progress on this front So we will discuss this in detail In the lang chain It is very easy to make AI Now think for yourself

Will break down your query
